source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira
revision_note: First verified ingestion batch (2026-07-22). All chunks are verbatim excerpts from the named public-domain translation, cross-referenced against the cited sacred-texts.com URL, with short unquoted connective/context sentences clearly distinguishable from the quoted text. See docs/DHARAM_VEER_COVERAGE_AUDIT.md.

content.text: "...the Venerable Ascetic Mahavira, the last of the Tirthakaras...took the form of an embryo in the womb of Devananda...in the brahmanical part of the town Kundagrama." By the god Sakra's order, the embryo was then transferred to the womb of "the Kshatriyani Trisala...wife of the Kshatriya Siddhartha," since it is held that Arhats are never born from a brahmanical womb, only a noble Kshatriya one.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: "The Venerable Ascetic Mahavira belonged to the Kasyapa gotra. His three names have thus been recorded: by his parents he was called Vardhamana; because he is devoid of love and hate, he is called Sramana (i.e. Ascetic); because he stands fast in midst of dangers and fears, patiently bears hardships and calamities, adheres to the chosen rules of penance, is wise, indifferent to pleasure and pain, rich in control, and gifted with fortitude, the name Venerable Ascetic Mahavira has been given him by the gods."
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: At age thirty, after his parents' death, Mahavira renounced the world: "He left his silver, he left his gold, he left his riches, corn, majesty, and kingdom...he distributed presents through proper persons, he distributed presents among indigent persons." He then "descended from his palankin, took off his ornaments, garlands, and finery with his own hands, and with his own hands plucked out his hair in five handfuls," fasting two and a half days before entering "the state of houselessness."
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: "The Venerable Ascetic Mahavira for a year and a month wore clothes; after that time he walked about naked, and accepted the alms in the hollow of his hand. For more than twelve years the Venerable Ascetic Mahavira neglected his body and abandoned the care of it; he with equanimity bore, underwent, and suffered all pleasant or unpleasant occurrences arising from divine powers, men, or animals."
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: Of his equanimity through those years of austerity: "his heart was pure like the water...in autumn; nothing could soil him like the leaf of a lotus; his senses were well protected like those of a tortoise; he was single and alone like the horn of a rhinoceros; he was free like a bird...strong like a bull, difficult to attack like a lion, steady and firm like Mount Mandara, deep like the ocean, mild like the moon...pure like excellent gold...like a well-kindled fire he shone in his splendour."
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: After thirteen years of ascetic practice, "outside of the town Grimbhikagrama on the bank of the river Rigupalika...under a Sal tree...in a squatting position with joined heels, exposing himself to the heat of the sun, after fasting two and a half days without drinking water, being engaged in deep meditation, (he) reached the highest knowledge and intuition, called Kevala, which is infinite, supreme, unobstructed, unimpeded, complete, and full." As a Kevalin, the text says he then "knew and saw all conditions of the world."
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: Mahavira taught for thirty more years before dying at Pava. The text records his community at the time of his death as "an excellent community of fourteen thousand Sramanas...thirty-six thousand nuns...one hundred and fifty-nine thousand lay votaries...three hundred and eighteen thousand female lay votaries," among other named ranks of advanced disciples — the fourfold Jain community (monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen) that continues today.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

content.text: Mahavira (Vardhamana), the 24th and last Tirthakara of this era in Jain tradition, is the subject of the Kalpa Sutra's Life of Mahavira (Lectures 1-5). This entry draws only on Lecture 2 (conception) and Lecture 5 (naming, renunciation, austerities, enlightenment, death, community) of Hermann Jacobi's 1884 translation, Sacred Books of the East vol. 22, public domain. The Kalpa Sutra's earlier lectures and its separate Life of Parsva are not reproduced here.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira
